title: Beyond dataset_catalog: Automating Cross-Catalog Dashboard Promotion in Databricks with DABs and…
page_content: Beyond dataset_catalog: Automating Cross-Catalog Dashboard Promotion in Databricks with DABs and GitHub ActionsRohit Chamarthi9 min read·2 hours ago--ListenSharedataset_catalog solved 80% of the problem. Here’s the pattern for the other 20%, and it’s the 20% that enterprise teams actually get stuck on.Read it on friend linkPress enter or click to view image in full sizeIf you’ve promoted a Databricks dashboard from dev to production recently, you’ve probably done some version of this: open the dashboard in dev, download the .lvdash.json file, find-and-replace every catalog reference from marketing_dev_catalog to marketing_prod_catalog, upload it to the prod workspace, reconnect the warehouse, and hope nothing broke in the process.Now multiply that by five dashboards, three environments, and a team of four analysts who all think “the other person already updated QA.”That was us six months ago. We were spending more time promoting dashboards than building them. So we automated the entire pipeline, and in the process, ran into a gap that none of the existing documentation or blog posts address: what happens when a single dashboard queries across multiple catalogs?This article walks through the complete solution we built, covering both the straightforward case (one catalog per dashboard) and the harder case (cross-catalog dashboards). Everything runs on Declarative Automation Bundles (formerly Asset Bundles), GitHub Actions, and zero manual intervention.The StackBefore we get into the how, here’s what we’re working with:Declarative Automation Bundles(DABs) Infrastructure-as-code for Databricks resources. Defines dashboards, variables, and deployment targets in YAML.GitHub + GitHub Actions Source control and CI/CD. Branches map to environments (dev → qa → main/prod).Databricks CLI (v0.281.0+) The version matters. The dataset_catalog and dataset_schema fields we rely on were only introduced in this release.Service Principals (OAuth M2M) Non-human identities for automated deployments.Unity Catalog The catalog layer that makes environment-specific data isolation possible.Genie Code for dahsboard authoring (Dashboard Authoring Agent)Press enter or click to view image in full sizeThe end-to-end flow: author in Dev, configure the bundle, promote through Git branches, GitHub Actions deploys to each workspace with the correct catalog contextThe Environment SetupThis example uses two business domains: Sales and Operations, each with their own catalog per environment:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeEach catalog contains an analytics schema with the same table structure.Pattern 1: dataset_catalog (The 80% Solution)If every dataset in your dashboard hits the same catalog, Databricks has you covered. As of CLI v0.281.0, the dashboard resource in DABs supports two fields: dataset_catalog and dataset_schema. These set the default catalog and schema context for all datasets in the dashboard at deploy time, without modifying the .lvdash.json.The key requirement: your dashboard SQL must use unqualified table names.Press enter or click to view image in full sizeDrop the catalog prefix. Let dataset_catalog handle itThe bundle configuration makes this work. Define variables for the catalog and schema, override them per target, and reference them in the dashboard resource:Three targets, one bundle. The catalog name changes per environment, but the dashboard SQL doesn’t (databricks.yml)variables:  dashboard_catalog:    description: "Catalog hosting the dashboard views"    default: sales_dev_catalog  dashboard_schema:    description: "Schema containing dashboard views"    default: dashboard_views  warehouse_id:    description: "SQL Warehouse ID"targets:  dev:    mode: development    default: true    workspace:      host: https://dev-workspace.cloud.databricks.com    variables:      dashboard_catalog: sales_dev_catalog      warehouse_id: "dev-warehouse-id"  qa:    workspace:      host: https://qa-workspace.cloud.databricks.com    variables:      dashboard_catalog: sales_qa_catalog      warehouse_id: "qa-warehouse-id"  prod:    workspace:      host: https://prod-workspace.cloud.databricks.com    variables:      dashboard_catalog: sales_pd_catalog      warehouse_id: "prod-warehouse-id"Six lines. That’s all it takes to make a dashboard environment-aware (/resources/sales_operations_performance.dashboard.yml)resources:  dashboards:    sales_operations_performance:      display_name: "Sales & Operations Performance"      file_path: ../src/sales_operations_performance.lvdash.json      warehouse_id: ${var.warehouse_id}      dataset_catalog: ${var.dashboard_catalog}      dataset_schema: ${var.dashboard_schema}We built our first dashboard, Sales & Operations Performance using a view abstraction layer. Views in each environment point to the underlying tables across catalogs, and the dashboard queries only reference view names. With dataset_catalog set per target, the same .lvdash.json deploys to all three environments without a single edit.This pattern works well when all your datasets live under one catalog. But what about dashboards that need data from multiple catalogs in a single query?The Gap — Cross-Catalog DashboardsHere’s where it gets interesting. Our executive dashboard joins Sales revenue data with Ops fulfillment metrics. In dev, that query looks like this:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeTwo different catalogs in one query. dataset_catalog can only set one default. Setting it to sales_dev_catalog would resolve the revenue table correctly, but the ops table would fail. There's no dataset_catalog_2 field.This is the gap. And if you search the Databricks docs, community forums, or existing Medium articles, you won’t find a documented solution. The GitHub CLI issue (#1915) has 20+ upvotes from teams hitting the same wall.Press enter or click to view image in full sizeWe weren’t the only ones stuck. GitHub issue #1915 has been open since November 2024.Pattern 2: IDENTIFIER() + Pipeline Transform (The Other 20%)The solution has two parts: a SQL pattern for the dashboard queries, and a pipeline step that handles the catalog swap.Part 1: The SQL PatternDatabricks SQL has an IDENTIFIER() function that takes a string and interprets it as a table reference. Combined with dashboard parameters, this lets you parameterize catalog names inside the query:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeTwo catalogs, one query. The :sales_cat and :ops_cat parameters are STRING values set as dashboard parameters with dev catalog defaultsThe parameters sales_cat and ops_cat are defined as STRING dashboard parameters with default values like sales_dev_catalog. These defaults live inside the .lvdash.json file.Part 2: The Pipeline TransformHere’s the design decision that matters. We could have built a Python script that lives in the developer’s repository to swap these defaults before deployment. But that crosses a line it’s a DevOps concern leaking into the developer’s codebase.Instead, we handle it entirely in the GitHub Actions workflow using sed and environment variables. The developer never sees it. Their code stays clean (simple, yet efficient).- name: Transform catalog references  if: ${{ needs.validate.outputs.target != 'dev' }}  working-directory: dashboard-dabs  run: |    echo "Transforming catalogs for ${{ needs.validate.outputs.target }}"    echo "  sales_dev_catalog → ${{ vars.SALES_CATALOG }}"    echo "  ops_dev_catalog   → ${{ vars.OPS_CATALOG }}"    find src/ -name "*.lvdash.json" -exec sed -i \      -e "s/sales_dev_catalog/${{ vars.SALES_CATALOG }}/g" \      -e "s/ops_dev_catalog/${{ vars.OPS_CATALOG }}/g" \      {} +The catalog swap happens in the pipeline, not in the code. SALES_CATALOG and OPS_CATALOG are GitHub environment variables — different values for QA and Prod.Press enter or click to view image in full sizeAll environment-specific values live in GitHub Environments. Adding a new catalog is a variable change, not a code changeAfter the transform swaps the catalog names, the bundle deploys normally. After deployment, a git checkout -- src/ resets the transformed files so the working tree stays clean.The resource YAML for this dashboard is deliberately simpler — no dataset_catalog, no dataset_schema. The catalogs are handled by the IDENTIFIER() parameters, not by DABs:resources:  dashboards:    executive_cross_catalog_dashboard:      display_name: "Executive Cross-Catalog Dashboard"      file_path: ../src/executive_cross_catalog_dashboard.lvdash.json      warehouse_id: ${var.warehouse_id}No dataset_catalog here. The catalog context comes from IDENTIFIER() parameters inside the SQL, swapped by the pipeline.The CI/CD PipelineThe full pipeline runs on GitHub Actions with a branch-per-environment strategy:dev branch → deploys to Dev workspace (auto on push)qa branch → deploys to QA workspace (on merge from dev)main branch → deploys to Prod workspace (on merge from qa)The pipeline covers validation, catalog transformation, and deployment. In a production setup, you’d add post-deploy smoke tests (hit the Lakeview API to confirm the dashboard renders), Slack/Teams notifications, and Git tagging for rollback. But those are additive — the core promotion pattern works without them.Press enter or click to view image in full sizeBranch-to-environment mapping. Each merge triggers validation and deployment to the corresponding workspaceA single workflow file handles all three environments. The branch name determines which target to deploy to, and GitHub Environments provide the credentials and catalog variables:name: Deploy Dashboardson:  push:    branches: [dev, qa, main]jobs:  validate:    runs-on: ubuntu-latest    environment: ${{ github.ref_name == 'main' && 'prod' || github.ref_name }}    steps:      - uses: actions/checkout@v4      - uses: databricks/setup-cli@main        with:          version: 0.291.0      - name: Map branch to target        id: set-target        run: |          case "${{ github.ref_name }}" in            dev)  echo "target=dev" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT ;;            qa)   echo "target=qa" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT ;;            main) echo "target=prod" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT ;;          esac      - name: Validate bundle        working-directory: dashboard-dabs        env:          DATABRICKS_HOST: ${{ vars.DATABRICKS_HOST }}          DATABRICKS_CLIENT_ID: ${{ secrets.DATABRICKS_CLIENT_ID }}          DATABRICKS_CLIENT_SECRET: ${{ secrets.DATABRICKS_CLIENT_SECRET }}        run: databricks bundle validate -t ${{ steps.set-target.outputs.target }}  deploy:    needs: validate    runs-on: ubuntu-latest    environment: ${{ needs.validate.outputs.target }}    steps:      - uses: actions/checkout@v4      - uses: databricks/setup-cli@main        with:          version: 0.291.0      - name: Transform catalog references        if: ${{ needs.validate.outputs.target != 'dev' }}        working-directory: dashboard-dabs        run: |          find src/ -name "*.lvdash.json" -exec sed -i \            -e "s/sales_dev_catalog/${{ vars.SALES_CATALOG }}/g" \            -e "s/ops_dev_catalog/${{ vars.OPS_CATALOG }}/g" \            {} +      - name: Deploy bundle        working-directory: dashboard-dabs        env:          DATABRICKS_HOST: ${{ vars.DATABRICKS_HOST }}          DATABRICKS_CLIENT_ID: ${{ secrets.DATABRICKS_CLIENT_ID }}          DATABRICKS_CLIENT_SECRET: ${{ secrets.DATABRICKS_CLIENT_SECRET }}        run: databricks bundle deploy -t ${{ needs.validate.outputs.target }} --force      - name: Reset transformed files        if: ${{ needs.validate.outputs.target != 'dev' }}        run: git checkout -- src/Seeing It in ActionHere’s the promotion in action in the Prod environment:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeThe transform step swaps catalog names in under a second. The deploy pushes the dashboard to the target workspacePress enter or click to view image in full sizeThe Executive Cross-Catalog Dashboard open in the Prod workspace with parameterized cross-catalog referencesTwo Patterns & When to Use WhichPress enter or click to view image in full sizeFor most teams, dataset_catalog covers 80% of dashboards. Keep it as your default. Reach for IDENTIFIER() only when a single dashboard genuinely needs data from multiple catalogs, typically cross-functional executive dashboards.The view abstraction layer is a third option worth considering. Create views in a single catalog that point to tables across multiple catalogs. The dashboard queries only reference view names, and dataset_catalog handles the rest. This is the cleanest approach for teams that can manage the view layer, but it adds a maintenance surface.A Note to the Databricks Product TeamThe dataset_catalog feature was a direct response to community feedback. GitHub issue #1915 had 20+ upvotes from teams stuck on exactly this problem. It solved the single-catalog case well.But as organizations adopt Data Mesh patterns with domain-oriented catalogs, cross-catalog dashboards are becoming the norm. Here’s what we think the next iteration could look like:Per-dataset catalog overrides — Allow dataset_catalog to be set at the dataset level, not just the dashboard level. Different datasets in the same dashboard could reference different catalogs.Variable substitution in .lvdash.json — Let DABs variables resolve inside the dashboard JSON, specifically in parameter default values. This would eliminate the pipeline transform entirely.Catalog aliases — A mapping layer where developers write SELECT * FROM sales.analytics.revenue and the deploy config maps sales to sales_dev_catalog in dev, sales_qa_catalog in QA, etc.Each of these builds on the existing pattern. The first one is a natural extension of what already works.Getting Started!The complete source code is available on GitHub:Press enter or click to view image in full sizeDevelopers own dashboard-dabs/. DevOps owns .github/workflows/. Clean separationPrerequisites:Databricks CLI v0.281.0+ (for dataset_catalog support)Three Databricks workspaces (dev, qa, prod) with Unity Catalog enabledA Service Principal per workspace with workspace access and SQL warehouse permissionsA GitHub repo with three branches: dev, qa, mainGitHub Environments configured with workspace URLs and SP credentialsThe end-to-end setup, from empty repo to production dashboard took us about half a day. Most of that was the initial environment setup and permissions. Once the pipeline is wired, promoting a new dashboard is a git push and two pull requests.Ship It!Dashboard promotion shouldn’t be a manual process. With DABs, GitHub Actions, and a clear separation between the developer’s code and the DevOps pipeline, you can go from dev to production without touching a single catalog name, downloading a single file, or logging into a single workspace.If you’re hitting the same cross-catalog wall we did, the IDENTIFIER() + pipeline transform pattern fills the gap until Databricks ships a native solution. And if you’re on the Databricks product team reading this — issue #1915 is still waiting.The full code is on GitHub. Fork it, adapt it, ship it.
